nativeRange.habitat: Rocky and coral reefs, typically at 3–70 m depth; juveniles often shelter in branching corals while adults patrol open reef structure and forage over sand and rubble.

diet.notes: Feeds on a variety of meaty foods. In the wild, adults are documented eating brittle stars, crustaceans, molluscs, and sea urchins, while juveniles act as cleaners removing parasites from larger fish. In captivity, generally accepts mysis, frozen krill, chopped shellfish, squid, and other meaty marine preparations. Tends to forage actively over rock and substrate.

summary: A striking Western Atlantic wrasse with a deep purple-blue upper body and vivid yellow underside. Juveniles act as cleaner fish; adults tend to become semi-aggressive and prey on small invertebrates.
careNotes:
The Spanish Hogfish is generally considered an intermediate-level marine fish. Juveniles are typically peaceful and have been observed removing parasites from tankmates, but as they mature they tend to become semi-aggressive and have been known to bully smaller, more docile species. They are commonly considered incompatible with reef invertebrates — snails, ornamental shrimp, small crabs, worms, clams, and sea urchins are at risk — so this species is generally best housed in a fish-only or fish-only-with-live-rock (FOWLR) system rather than a reef tank.

This species is described as an active swimmer that benefits from ample open water column space alongside rocky hiding places and caves. It is generally regarded as hardy and disease-resistant.

Tank size disagreement across sources: LiveAquaria recommends 150 gal (568 L), FreshMarine recommends 155 gal (586 L) for adults, and ReefApp recommends 1500 L (~396 gal). Given the wide spread (>2×) and the species' potential 40 cm adult size, active swimming behavior, and predatory disposition, 700 L has been selected as a cautious round-number estimate balancing retailer and specialist guidance. Reviewers may wish to reconsider.

Temperature disagreement: LiveAquaria and FreshMarine list 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C); FishBase records wild habitat at 22–28°C (preferred 23.5–28°C). Averaged across sources, roughly 23–27°C.

Size disagreement: LiveAquaria lists 10 in (25 cm); FreshMarine lists up to 16 in (40 cm), typical 10–13 in; FishBase records 40 cm TL max with common length 28 cm. The 25–40 cm range reflects typical-to-maximum reported size.

This species is a protogynous hermaphrodite and in the wild forms harems of one dominant male with several smaller females; sex reversal completes in approximately 7–10 days.
breedingNotes: Reported to be oviparous with pelagic eggs and larvae; pair-spawns at dusk. As a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite, forms harem groups with one male and several females. Reliable home-aquarium breeding has not been documented.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Product page with care sheet. Listed minimum tank 150 gal, temperature 72–78°F, SG 1.020–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, max size 10 in, care level easy, temperament aggressive toward smaller fish.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Labridae, Eupercaria), max size (40 cm TL, common 28 cm), native range (Western Atlantic, Bermuda/Florida to southern Brazil), depth (1–70 m), wild temperature 22–28°C, and confirmed protogynous hermaphrodite with harem grouping. FishBase temperature is somewhat warmer than LiveAquaria's 72–78°F; averaged across sources.

additional.name: ReefApp
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (40 cm), reef safety ('with caution' / invertebrate hunter), and tank volume (1500 L). Tank size is substantially larger than LiveAquaria's 150 gal / 568 L; the >2× spread triggered needs_review.

additional.name: FreshMarine
additional.notes: Additional cross-check: minimum tank 155 gal for adults, temperature 72–78°F, SG 1.020–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, max size up to 16 in, care level moderate, semi-aggressive, reef safe with caution.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been reliably documented. Oviparous with pelagic eggs; protogynous hermaphrodite that forms harems in the wild.
